Q1: I tried to organize my study routine around my extracurricular activities and other commitments, which honestly made things pretty chaotic. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so I needed to actually do things hands-on rather than just read, which meant my study sessions were longer but less frequent. I'd block out chunks of time on weekends and a couple of evenings, aiming for about 22 hours a week total. But with my attendance being so inconsistent, I often felt like I was playing catch-up rather than staying ahead.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were coming up. I tried to keep some regularity, but between my extracurriculars and other responsibilities, I'd often have to shift things around. When assignments were due, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks, I might slack off a bit. It wasn't ideal, but I did what I could with the time I had.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was either an upcoming deadline or getting a notification about new materials being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed since my attendance was so spotty. I also used the online courses I was enrolled in as a way to supplement what I was missing in class. Fear of falling behind was honestly a big motivator for me to check in.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging into the LMS became more routine, especially early in the semester when I was determined to stay on top of things. I'd check it in the morning with my coffee before heading out. But that routine fell apart pretty quickly when life got busier and my anxiety started creeping up. It never really became a solid habit that stuck throughout the whole course.
Q5: The hands-on activities and any interactive elements were the most useful for me because of how I learn. I also found the discussion posts helpful because engaging with other students helped concepts click better than just reading alone. The video content was decent too, though I often had to rewatch sections multiple times. Anything that let me actively participate rather than passively consume felt more valuable to my learning.
Q6: I often ignored the longer reading materials and some of the supplementary resources that weren't directly tied to assignments. If something seemed optional or too text-heavy, I'd put it off indefinitely. Time was always tight, so I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades. Looking back, maybe I missed some important context, but I was just trying to survive.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams, or if it helped me understand something I was struggling with. I also valued activities that let me physically engage with the material somehow, like practice problems or interactive simulations. If it felt like busy work or too abstract, I'd lose interest quickly. The immediate payoff mattered a lot to me.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, even though I knew they might help. Required activities had urgency attached to them, so they automatically got priority in my schedule. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later never really came. There was this constant guilt about not doing them, but the fear of failing required work was stronger.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download materials and work through them on my tablet while commuting or during breaks at my extracurricular activities. I also used external YouTube videos and other resources to help explain concepts in different ways. My notes from those sessions were extensive, but none of that activity was tracked anywhere online.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or practicing concepts with physical materials that the LMS couldn't capture. I'd sometimes study with classmates in person, going over downloaded slides and working through examples together. I also did a lot of mental reviewing while doing other activities, like going over concepts in my head during exercise. The LMS only saw a fraction of my actual study time.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach for a lot of the course materials. I'd access something once, download it or take screenshots, and then work with it offline for days or even weeks. It was more convenient for my schedule since I wasn't always somewhere with reliable internet. The LMS would show one brief login, but I might have spent hours with that material afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard for me to stay engaged. My anxiety was spiking, and I started feeling scared about my performance despite putting in the work. My attendance dropped even more because I felt embarrassed showing up after missing so much. The CBT sessions I was doing helped me manage some of the fear, but it was still a struggle to face the course head-on.
Q13: Definitely, all of those factors played a role at different times. When I was exhausted from my extracurriculars, I'd log in but barely absorb anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections of the LMS entirely because facing them felt overwhelming. Competing responsibilities meant I'd sometimes have to choose between the LMS and other pressing obligations, and the LMS often lost.
Q14: There were plenty of times I'd have the LMS open but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be watching a video but thinking about everything I was scared about failing or what I needed to do next. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but nothing was actually sinking in. It was engagement in appearance only.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation a lot because the thought of facing how much I didn't know terrified me. Even though I completed almost all my assignments, I'd avoid the deeper studying that would have helped my exam scores. It was this weird contradiction where I was diligent about submissions but avoidant about truly mastering the material. The fear of confronting my gaps kept me in a cycle of surface-level engagement.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS usage. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, logging in multiple times to make sure I submitted everything correctly. Without a deadline, my engagement would drop significantly. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and get me moving.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would motivate me for a short while, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to withdraw and avoid the course. Given my exam scores weren't great despite my assignment completion, I started dreading feedback altogether. The CBT sessions helped me reframe some of that, but it was still emotionally difficult to engage after receiving disappointing results.
Q18: The course structure felt a bit rigid for someone with my attendance issues and learning style. I needed more flexibility and hands-on opportunities than what was offered. The weekly modules were predictable, which helped in some ways, but when I fell behind, it felt impossible to catch up within that structure. I would have benefited from more alternative pathways to demonstrate my learning.
Q19: The heavy reliance on video lectures and reading made engagement harder for me as a kinesthetic learner. I needed to do things, not just watch or read about them. The discussion posts were actually helpful because they required active participation. But overall, the design assumed a type of learner I'm not, which created an extra barrier I had to work around constantly.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying really hard to understand but struggling to produce quality work. They might be watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, searching for help, but still not grasping things well enough to submit confident work. It could also be anxiety-related, where they're engaging with materials but too scared to commit to submitting something that might fail. Activity doesn't always equal understanding or confidence.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat, though my exam scores don't reflect it well. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in my LMS activity. Someone could absolutely understand the material through external resources, study groups, or downloaded content and perform decently without much visible LMS engagement. The platform doesn't capture everything that contributes to learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when someone logs in, what they click on, and whether they submit things on time. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture whether someone was actually focused or just going through the motions. They also miss the emotional and psychological factors that influence how someone interacts with the material.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's about being mentally present and curious, even when things are difficult. For me, it also means finding ways to physically interact with concepts, not just passively consuming information. True engagement is when learning feels meaningful, not just mandatory.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I struggled with fear and anxiety throughout this course, or how hard I worked in ways that weren't trackable. It wouldn't capture my offline study sessions, my discussions with classmates outside the platform, or the mental energy I spent trying to overcome my avoidance. It also wouldn't show that despite failing, I never stopped trying to climb back up and figure things out.
